summary: I am originally from Rwanda, a country where I was born and raised. In 1994, during a time of war and genocide, I had to leave Rwanda for my safety. I returned for two years after the genocide stopped, but due to ongoing insecurity, I had to leave again in 1998. Rwanda is predominantly Christian, with a strong Catholic presence, which shaped my upbringing and faith deeply.

The 1994 Rwandan genocide was a horrific event that forced me to flee my country. During that time, many of my family members and friends were killed, and I witnessed unimaginable violence. I survived by relying on my faith and prayers, especially carrying a rosary given by my mother. Although the genocide ended, the aftermath left Rwanda unsafe, prompting me to leave again in 1998.

After fleeing Rwanda in 1994, I spent two years in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. It was a time of survival and uncertainty, as I was escaping the war and genocide in my homeland. The Congo was my first refuge before I returned briefly to Rwanda and then embarked on a longer journey through several countries.

Uganda was one of the countries I passed through after leaving Rwanda again in 1998. My journey was long and difficult, traveling mostly by foot and bus, and Uganda was part of that route. Although I did not have much opportunity to join religious communities during this time, my faith remained a constant source of strength.

Kenya was another country I traveled through on my way from Rwanda to South Africa and eventually Europe. Like Uganda, it was part of my long and challenging journey as a refugee. Despite the hardships, I kept my rosary with me and prayed for safety and guidance.

Mozambique was one of the countries I passed through during my escape from Rwanda. The journey was physically demanding and often dangerous, and I had little chance to participate in church communities there. My faith was mostly a personal practice during this time as I focused on survival.

In South Africa, specifically in Cape Town's Pinelands neighborhood, I was able to join a Christian community for the first time in my journey. Although I was Catholic, I worshipped alongside other denominations like Baptist and Presbyterian, focusing on the shared faith rather than differences. However, the refugee system was disorganized, and it took me two years to get proper documents, which made life difficult and led me to seek opportunities in Europe.

Germany was where I finally found stability and a welcoming Catholic community after my long journey. I lived near Frankfurt and later in another city where my children were baptized. The church community was very supportive, helping me feel welcomed and cared for, and they assisted me with important documents like my son's birth certificate even years later.

The United States was my final destination after leaving Germany. I came here to continue my studies and build a new life after years of displacement. My journey to the US marked the end of a long and difficult path through multiple countries, and it gave me hope for a more secure future.

I was born and raised in a Catholic family in Rwanda, attending Catholic schools through grade twelve. The Catholic Church played a central role in my life, especially during difficult times like the genocide and my journey as a refugee. Even when I was unable to attend church regularly, I carried my rosary and prayed constantly. Later, in Germany, I reconnected with a Catholic community that supported me and my family.

The rosary has been a constant companion throughout my life, especially during the darkest moments. My mother gave me a rosary when I was young, and I carried it in my pocket during the war and my escape. Whenever I faced danger or despair, I would take out my rosary and pray for my family and friends. It symbolizes my faith and connection to Jesus, and it has helped me survive and find peace.

My name, Akeyezu, means 'someone who belongs to Jesus' in Rwanda, which reflects my family's deep faith. Jesus has always been central to my life and decisions, guiding me through hardship and loss. I was raised Catholic, and my relationship with Jesus has given me strength, especially during the genocide and my years as a refugee. I believe that God has led me and answered my prayers throughout my journey.

While I was raised Catholic, my younger brother attended a Baptist church school, so I was familiar with Baptist Christianity growing up in Rwanda. Later, during my travels, I worshipped alongside Baptist Christians in places like South Africa. For me, the specific denomination was less important than the shared faith and community in prayer and worship.

During my time in South Africa, I joined Christian communities that included Presbyterians as well as Baptists and Catholics. I found that despite some differences in interpretation, the core of worship and faith was very similar. I did not feel discriminated against as a Catholic and valued the unity in prayer regardless of denomination.

Pinelands in Cape Town was where I first joined a Christian church community after fleeing Rwanda. A friend named Mary took me to her church there, and I prayed with that group until I left South Africa. It was a meaningful place for me to reconnect with communal worship after a long period of just personal prayer during my travels.

I lived in a small city about 20 miles east of Frankfurt when I first arrived in Germany. This was where I joined a Catholic community and began to rebuild my life. The church and people there welcomed me warmly, showing interest in my story and supporting my family.

In Germany, I met a priest from Nigeria who was part of the Catholic community that supported me and my family. He and other parishioners expressed sympathy for what happened in Rwanda and were curious about my story. Their kindness helped me feel welcomed and cared for during a difficult time.
The transcript does not mention the University of Louisville, so I have no personal experience or story to share about it.
